noteGrp.note: As there is room for another letter before and before , I read and . Land’s text is unintelligible.
There is room for another letter before , and we should probably insert .
παράδεισος.
Read for .
φακιόλιον (Brockelmann ποικίλη).
In the lost beginning of the chapter(?).
The word generally expresses metropolitan jurisdiction, so that the expression is equivalent to "in the province of Helenopontus.''
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
φιλοτιμία.
. I can find no parallel for this use of the word in the lexicons, but it may perhaps be so rendered at p. 213, l. 10 (L.).
ἐγκυκλία.
There is perhaps a gap here, as Land marks, though it is not certain that there ever was anything written there. So also in the next line.
ἐγκυκλία.
λαυρᾶτον.
Deut. xxviii. 15.
Deut. xxviii. 24-26.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.

. The adverb occurs in 1. 1 (p. 3, l. 22, L.), 7. 6 (p. 215, l. 2), and 7. 9 (p. 224, l. 13); see p. 13, note 6. Here, by a slight alteration of meaning, we may render it "violent" or "stormy," a meaning supported by the expression (stormy water?), which is quoted by Payne Smith from the Lexicon of Bar ʿAli. We might, however, read , "thick," as proposed by Smith on 7. 9. The adjective also occurs in 1. 7 (p. 73, 1. 5) as an epithet of words, where the meaning seems to be "heated," "violent."
Summer 553.
Read for .
I read for , as Dr. Hamilton suggests.
ἀποσχίσται.
Read for .
This perhaps represents the Teutonic Audwin or Aldwin ; or we might make the easy correction for , and translate "Evodian."
The MS. has .£0.^0X3, not .Ca^.JQjO, as L. prints.
It is probable that something has here dropped out.
οὐσία.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Insert before .

In the lost portion. After this it is probable that something has been lost, since the following statements can hardly apply to John. In this case we may render "of whom we have mentioned that […, and X.,] who was providentially present."
By "πρόνοια."
The MS. has , not , as L.
σκάριφος.
Elʿazar is meant.
Ptol. Geog. vi. 7. 21.
Idid. vi. 7. 23.
Similarly in place of "τῶν Ἀσαβῶν ὀρῶν" (vi. 7. 24) he has "the mountains of Ofir."
Ptol. Geog. iv. 9. 3.
Ibid. vi. 7. 11. Montanus prints it as part of the text, and so it is in the Athos MS. reproduced by Langlois. Wilberg brackets it.
Ibid. iv. 7. 31.
Ibid.
Ibid. vi. 7. 23. "Δωρηνοί" is the reading of nearly all the MSS.

So in the case of the tribe who in Nobbe's text are called Ὀμαγκῖται, and in Montanus' Ὀμανῖται, our author's reading, (14,620, ), though corrupt, is nearer to the latter than the former, and nearer still to Ὀμαμῖται, which is read by several MSS.
Ibid. vii. 4. 1.
The MS. has ^QAjJ, not vQ-i>!J. as L. prints.
Isa. ii. 4.
Probably the word "first" has dropped out.
I.e. Georgia or Iberia.
Read for .
I.e. Abasgia.
This is probably a corruption or confusion, as the Bulgarians are said above to have had cities. The people here meant are perhaps the "Βουρούγουνδοι" of Agath. v. 11.
Cf. Strabo, xi. 5. 1.
The MS. has, not , as L. prints.
. This should mean "cut limb from limb."
The MS. has before , which L. does not print.
From Armenian kardal, to call, and Astuats, God.
MS. apparently , certainly not , as L.
MS. , without division; there is no before it, as L.
Insert before .
Insert before .
πρεσβεία.
Perhaps we should read , "from him."
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Insert before .
Rom. xi. 25.
Omit before .
The MS. reading seems to be , for which we must read , as L.
Only is visible, but the word must be , as no other word beginning with those letters suits the context.
L. prints only ; the second letter seems to be : read .
The MS. has , not , as L. prints.
Only is visible. I guess at the meaning. Perhaps .
ξενοδοχεῖον (text ξενόδοχος).
possibly, as Payne Smith suggests, = κλῆρος; or perhaps κελλάριον, "store-room," might stand for "stores."
The MS. has before , not , as L. prints.

. At this point the MS. breaks off, which makes it hard to tell the meaning of this word. The ordinary meaning "division" or "half" seems impossible, and I therefore take it to be the other which perhaps = φάλαγξ: cf. Bedjan, Act. Mart, et Sanct. vol. ii. p. 540, l. 10, where James, the Persian martyr, is said to have been in the at court. Across the last page of the MS. some illegible words are written in another hand.
